primary_diagnosis.name: Symptomatic abdominal aortic aneurysm (suspected rapidly expanding, at risk of rupture)

pathophysiology_summary: Chronic degeneration and weakening of the infrarenal aortic wall (media elastin loss, atherosclerosis, sustained hypertensive wall stress) produces progressive aneurysmal dilatation. Once the aneurysm becomes symptomatic — new deep abdominal/back pain, a palpable expansile pulsatile mass, and tenderness — the wall is under acute strain and expansion or contained/frank rupture is imminent. Rupture causes catastrophic retroperitoneal or intraperitoneal haemorrhage with rapid exsanguination; even borderline tachycardia, pallor and presyncope signal early haemodynamic compromise. Definitive assessment requires CT angiography and emergency vascular surgery; the survival determinant is time to operative repair, so recognition and immediate escalation — not remote confirmation — are what matter.

differential.evidence_basis: Older hypertensive patient with new deep central abdominal pain radiating to the back and a felt pulsation — the classic symptomatic-AAA pattern until proven otherwise.
Back pain is common and benign in most patients — but is an anchoring trap here; the felt pulsation and non-mechanical nature argue against it.
Can cause severe flank/abdominal pain, but the pain here is central and constant rather than colicky unilateral loin-to-groin.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At opening, a competent telehealth clinician must hold symptomatic AAA at the top of the list for an older hypertensive with this pain pattern and NOT anchor on benign back pain.

differential.evidence_basis: Felt abdominal pulsation + deep constant central/back pain + presyncope on standing + known hypertension form a high-risk cluster.
Another catastrophic aortic emergency that can present with back pain and haemodynamic change; cannot be excluded remotely and reinforces urgent escalation.
Retained as an alternative but the constant, non-colicky, central character makes it less likely.
Can cause abdominal pain but usually left-lower-quadrant with fever and altered bowel habit — none reported.
Downgraded once the pain is confirmed non-mechanical and a pulsation is reported.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The pulsation and presyncope should promote AAA and dissection while demoting the benign mimics; failure to do so is premature closure.

differential.diagnosis: Symptomatic / rapidly expanding abdominal aortic aneurysm
differential.evidence_basis: Red-flag cluster (felt pulsation, worsening pain, presyncope, pallor) indicates a symptomatic aneurysm at risk of rupture — time-critical.

differential.diagnosis: Contained or frank ruptured abdominal aortic aneurysm
differential.evidence_basis: Presyncope, pallor and worsening pain may already reflect early/contained rupture — the reason self-transport is unsafe.
Remains on the differential; management (immediate emergency assessment) is the same.

differential_progression.scoring_note: By this stage the only safe disposition is emergency ambulance transfer; keeping benign mimics as the leading explanation is a critical under-triage.

differential.diagnosis: Symptomatic abdominal aortic aneurysm, suspected rapidly expanding — presumptive, pending CT angiography
differential.evidence_basis: Definitive confirmation and sizing require CT angiography and vascular review; the telehealth task is recognition and immediate escalation, not confirmation.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Correct final action: presume symptomatic AAA, call 000, keep supine and nil by mouth, and arrange emergency vascular assessment — do not attempt to confirm or exclude remotely.

red_flags.description: Pulsatile, expansile abdominal mass (felt by the patient; palpated supra-umbilically by an in-person clinician)
red_flags.clinical_significance: A pulsatile expansile mass with pain is the hallmark of a symptomatic aortic aneurysm.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missing it allows attribution to a benign cause and fatal delay to surgery.

red_flags.description: Deep, constant central abdominal pain radiating to the back, steadily worsening
red_flags.clinical_significance: New, non-mechanical, worsening abdominal/back pain in an older hypertensive is a symptomatic-AAA presentation until excluded.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Under-triage to routine back-pain or renal-colic pathways delays life-saving intervention.

red_flags.clinical_significance: Suggests early haemodynamic compromise / hypovolaemia from expansion or contained leak.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: May be the only warning of impending circulatory collapse; ignoring it risks collapse during self-transport.

red_flags.description: Pallor with borderline tachycardia (early shock physiology)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Pallor and a rising heart rate with a low-normal blood pressure indicate compensated haemorrhagic shock.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Compensated shock can decompensate abruptly; failure to escalate by ambulance is dangerous.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Attributing deep back pain in an older adult to musculoskeletal strain and advising analgesia/rest, missing a symptomatic aneurysm.
Settling on a common abdominal mimic before considering the catastrophic vascular cause.
A low-normal blood pressure (105/65) and only borderline tachycardia can look reassuring but represent compensated shock in a hypertensive patient whose baseline is higher.
Because the mass cannot be palpated remotely, wrongly downgrading urgency instead of escalating on the patient-reportable red-flag pattern.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Palpation of a pulsatile expansile abdominal mass
Accurate blood pressure and heart rate measurement
Assessment for peritonism / guarding, limb mottling, Grey Turner's or Cullen's sign
Aortic diameter and rupture status (CT angiography)
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Call 000 for an emergency ambulance now, keep the patient strictly supine and nil by mouth, and arrange immediate emergency-department transfer for CT angiography and vascular surgical review. Do not attempt remote confirmation and do not advise self-transport.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: The diagnosis itself is a presumption pending imaging; the dominant risk is UNDER-action — attributing the presentation to a benign mimic or advising a next-day GP/imaging review instead of an ambulance.
